choices: I want to climb a mountain.
Mountain climbing is dangerous but rewarding.
questions.explanation: States position the paragraph can develop.

questions.question: BEE : HIVE ::
questions.explanation: BEE lives in HIVE. BIRD lives in NEST. Animal to its specific built home.

wrongExplanations.0: Fish lives in water — habitat, not built structure.
wrongExplanations.2: Wolves don't live in forests as a structure.
wrongExplanations.3: Pasture is where cows graze, not a built home.

questions.question: Bus from 8:30 AM to 11:15 AM. Travel time?
Dr. King, an advocate of nonviolent protest, never ______ his belief in this resistance.

wrongExplanations.1: Means recognized — but "never acknowledged" contradicts being an advocate.

questions.question: All kittens are cats. All cats have whiskers. Therefore:
questions.explanation: Kittens → cats → whiskers. So kittens have whiskers.

Column B: 70 + 5

wrongExplanations.0: 77>75 → A is greater. Answer should be 0, not 1. Apologies for typo — A is greater.

questions.question:

What is the author's purpose?
choices: Persuade readers to support panda conservation

wrongExplanations.3: Brief description but main goal is action.

questions.passageText: Giant pandas are some of the most loved animals on Earth, yet there are only about 1,800 of them left in the wild. Pandas need bamboo forests to survive — and humans are cutting down those forests. We must protect the bamboo forests now. Buy products that don't harm wildlife. Support panda conservation. The future of pandas is in our hands.

questions.question: The teacher was ______ with the class because they had ______ on the test.
The ______ horse won the race despite the ______ weather.
questions.explanation: Fast horse won DESPITE bad weather.

wrongExplanations.3: Old + great = no contrast needed for despite.

questions.question: This sweater is ______ than that one.
What is the main idea of the whole passage?
choices: Coral reefs are important ocean homes that are now threatened
questions.explanation: Covers importance AND threats — both are central.
questions.passageText:
Coral reefs are sometimes called 'rainforests of the sea' because they support an incredible variety of ocean life. Though reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor, they are home to about 25% of all marine species.

Reefs are built by tiny animals called coral polyps. Over thousands of years, polyps build hard skeletons that grow into massive structures. Fish, turtles, sharks, and many other creatures live in and around these structures.

Today, coral reefs face serious problems. Warmer ocean temperatures cause coral 'bleaching,' which can kill the coral. Pollution and overfishing add to the threats. Many scientists worry that reefs may not survive without major changes.

questions.question: Mixed number for 7/4?
The ______ kitten ______ around the house.
PEN : INK ::
questions.explanation: PEN uses INK to write. PENCIL uses LEAD to write. Tool to the marking material it uses.

wrongExplanations.1: Wheel is part of a car.
wrongExplanations.2: Page is part of a book.
wrongExplanations.3: Smoke comes from fire — result, not writing material.

questions.question:
What comes next?
2, 4, 8, 16, ___
The garden was ______ in spring but ______ in winter.
Unlike her ______ older sister, Maya was outgoing and loved meeting new people.
Paragraph 2 is mainly about what?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 covers cells, hormones, immune system.
questions.passageText:
Every animal sleeps. Even fish. Why? For a long time, scientists weren't sure. But research has revealed several reasons.

First, sleep helps the body repair itself. Cells fix damage, growth hormones do their work, and the immune system gets stronger. Without enough sleep, you're more likely to get sick.

Second, sleep helps the brain. While you sleep, your brain sorts through what you learned that day. Memories get stored. New connections form. Without sleep, you can't focus, can't learn well, and can't remember.

Finally, sleep gives the brain time to clean itself. During sleep, fluid washes through the brain, removing waste from the day. Without this cleanup, brain damage builds up over time.

questions.question: Sam thinks his red shirt makes him score more goals. He wears it every game. Best test of his theory?
choices: Wear red half the time, blue half — compare goals
questions.explanation: Compare with vs. without red shirt.

questions.question: Order from smallest: 1/4, 1/2, 1/8, 1/3
questions.explanation: Smaller bottom = bigger fraction (with 1 on top).

questions.question: SCISSORS : CUT ::
questions.explanation: SCISSORS are used to CUT. PEN is used to WRITE. Tool to its action.

wrongExplanations.1: Cup HOLDS what you drink — you drink from it.
wrongExplanations.2: Plate HOLDS food — doesn't perform eating.
wrongExplanations.3: Mirror lets you see yourself — different relationship.

questions.question: GENEROUS : STINGY ::
questions.explanation: GENEROUS and STINGY are opposite traits. KIND and MEAN are opposite traits.

wrongExplanations.0: Happy and joyful mean the same.
wrongExplanations.1: Brave and bold mean the same.
wrongExplanations.3: Tall and huge are degrees, not opposites.

Use the pencil sometimes and a different pencil other times — compare scores

wrongExplanations.3: Different pencil isn't a fair test.

questions.question: Which is FACT, not opinion?
choices: Schools today give 15-20 minutes of recess
Our kids' health depends on it
School boards need to do this
questions.explanation: Schools' actual recess time is verifiable.
questions.passageText: Schools today give kids only 15 or 20 minutes of recess. That's not enough. Studies show that kids who have at least 30 minutes of recess each day do better in math and reading. They also have fewer behavior problems. School boards across the country need to make recess longer. Our kids' health and learning depend on it.

questions.question: Number of edges in a cube?
What does "He couldn't speak" at the end suggest?
questions.explanation: After being so worried, finding Buddy = overwhelmed.

wrongExplanations.2: OPPOSITE — he buried his face in joy.

questions.passageText: Marcus put up posters all over the neighborhood. "LOST DOG: Buddy. Brown lab. Last seen Tuesday." Three days passed with no calls. Marcus barely ate. He woke up at 3am thinking he heard barking. On the fourth night, the phone rang. "I think I have your dog," a woman said. Marcus drove to her house with shaking hands. When the front door opened, Buddy ran out, wagging his tail wildly. Marcus dropped to his knees and buried his face in the dog's fur. He couldn't speak.

What does "They didn't need to. The books said everything" suggest?
choices: They had a deep, quiet friendship through books
questions.explanation: Sharing books over years built a wordless bond.
questions.passageText: Lily loved Saturdays at the public library. She always sat in the same corner, by the window, with a stack of books beside her. The librarian, Mr. Owens, would nod hello and slide a new book onto her stack — always one she hadn't read but might love. He had been doing this since Lily was seven. Now she was twelve. They never said much. They didn't need to. The books said everything.

What comes next?
10, 9, 7, 4, ___
Distance from (1,1) to (4,5)?
What can you infer about Carmen's three months of practice?
questions.explanation: After the rough start, music flowed and teachers were impressed.
questions.passageText: Carmen had been practicing the violin for three months. The school orchestra audition was today. As she walked into the room, she saw three teachers sitting at a long table. "Whenever you're ready," Mr. Liu said kindly. Carmen lifted the violin to her chin. Her hands were shaking. She drew the bow across the strings. The first note came out wrong — sharp and squeaky. She paused, took a deep breath, and started over. This time the music flowed. By the end of her piece, the teachers were nodding. As she walked out, Carmen heard Mr. Liu say to another teacher, "She's got something."

questions.question: The students were ______ when the test was ______ until next week.
questions.explanation: Test moved = postponed. Less stress = relieved.

Some athletes are tall. All basketball players are athletes. Therefore:
choices: Some basketball players might be tall
All tall people are basketball players
questions.explanation: SOME athletes are tall. Basketball players are athletes. SOME might be tall.

wrongExplanations.2: Facts don't say anything about short.

questions.question: 8 students × 3 cookies each. Total?
What can you infer about Owen at mile 2?
questions.explanation: Gasping, burning legs, being passed = struggle, wanting to stop.

wrongExplanations.0: PARTIAL — but at mile 2 he's struggling.

questions.passageText: Owen had trained all year for the 5K race. As the runners lined up, his stomach twisted. There must have been 200 people in front of him at the starting line. The whistle blew. They were off. By mile 2, Owen was gasping. Runners were passing him. His legs burned. Then he saw a girl about his age, breathing hard but smiling, ahead of him. She glanced back, gave him a thumbs up, and shouted, "Almost there!" Owen pushed through the burn. He didn't win. He didn't even place. But when he crossed the finish line, his arms went up and his face broke into the biggest smile of his life.

wrongExplanations.0: Cause/effect — but the cat hides cause-and-effect-style. Both could work; meanwhile is more natural.

questions.question: Why did Lin give Maya the stone?
choices: She wanted to remember Maya by giving her something meaningful from their friendship
questions.explanation: From their park, where they built memories.

wrongExplanations.3: PARTIAL — it's about meaning, not preference.

questions.passageText: The moving truck was loaded. Maya stood in the empty front yard. Her best friend Lin walked up. Neither of them spoke for a long minute. "I'll text you every day," Lin finally said. "Me too," Maya whispered. Lin handed her something small wrapped in tissue paper. "For the trip." Maya unwrapped it slowly. It was a tiny stone — gray, smooth, ordinary. "From our park. Where we built that fort. So you have a piece of home." Maya bit her lip and pulled Lin into a hug.

questions.question: Paragraph 2 is mainly about what?
choices: Where penguins live and how they stay warm
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 covers location AND warmth (feathers, blubber).
questions.passageText:
Penguins are unusual birds — they cannot fly. But they are excellent swimmers. Their wings have evolved into flippers that work like fins underwater. A penguin can swim faster than most fish.

Most penguins live in the Southern Hemisphere, especially Antarctica. They survive bitter cold thanks to two layers: thick feathers on the outside and a layer of fat called blubber underneath.

Penguins live in large groups called colonies. Some colonies have hundreds of thousands of birds. They huddle together to stay warm in winter, taking turns moving from the cold edges to the warm center.

questions.question: The hurricane was so ______ that the entire coastal town had to ______.

wrongExplanations.3: Rebuilding before hurricane makes no sense.
